title: Merge Intervals
description: Given an array of intervals where `intervals[i] = [start_i, end_i]`, merge all overlapping intervals, and return an array of the non-overlapping intervals that cover all the intervals in the input.

parts.prompt: Given a list of intervals, merge all overlapping intervals and return the merged list.
parts.internalNotes: Sort by start time, then linear merge. Key insight: after sorting, you only need to compare the current interval with the last merged one. Common mistakes: not sorting first, using O(n²) pairwise comparison, off-by-one on overlap condition (touching intervals [1,2] and [2,3] should merge).

hints.content: What happens if you look at the intervals in a specific order?
Sort by start time first. Then think about when two consecutive intervals overlap.
Sort by start. Iterate: if current start <= last merged end, extend the merged interval. Otherwise start a new merged interval.

followUps: Is the sort stable? Does it matter?
parts.expectedApproach: Sort by start time, then single pass merge comparing with last merged interval.

transitionCriteria.transitionPrompt: Great. Now, given a set of meeting time intervals, determine the minimum number of conference rooms required.

parts.prompt: Given an array of meeting time intervals, find the minimum number of conference rooms required so that no two overlapping meetings share a room.
parts.internalNotes: Min-heap or sweep line. Sort events (start=+1, end=-1), scan and track max concurrent. Or sort by start, use a min-heap of end times. Common mistakes: not realizing this is different from merge intervals, trying to greedily assign rooms without a heap.

hints.content: Think about what happens at each moment in time — how many meetings are ongoing?
Consider a sweep line: what if you tracked meeting starts and ends as separate events?
Create events: +1 at each start, -1 at each end. Sort by time (break ties: end before start). Scan and track max concurrent count.

testCases.explanation: Sequential, no overlap (end=start counts as no overlap)

followUps: Could you use a min-heap approach instead?
parts.expectedApproach: Sweep line with +1/-1 events, or min-heap of end times.

io.inputFormat: int n, then n lines of 'start end'
io.outputFormat: Merged intervals, one per line as 'start end'
io.parseHint: First line: n. Next n lines: start end (space-separated).
